data.title: Private Credit Crisis Is Not The 2008 Subprime Crisis
data.description:
Earlier this week, the Federal Reserve reportedly asked US banks' about their exposure to private credit. Recent developments in the private credit market are compared by some analysts to the subprime market while other analysts downplay any impact. Which one makes more sense? In this article we will delve into whether the private credit market could turn out into another 2008 subprime crisis.

Hyman Minsky: The Theory Behind Financial Crises

For those of us that have been through the so-called Great Financial Crisis of 2008 (a period I remember as if it was still yesterday), Hyman Minsky was an economist that was widely quoted during that difficult period in time.

Without going into too many esoteric details, Hyman Minsky had many timeless observations about why financial cries were recurring.

Basically, he divided finance into three types:

  • 1.Interest and principal on debt can be fully repaid from cashflows.
  • 2. only the interest on debt can be fully repaid and the principal needs to be rolled over.
  • 3. neither the interest nor principal on debt can be repaid and relies on continuous borrowing or asset sales just to pay interest.

His critical insight, in my view, is a paradox: a stable economy creates the conditions for financial instability. In a stable economy, optimism grows and lenders and borrowers become complacent. Finance shifts from the first type to the second type and increasingly the third type. Success breeds more risk taking until you have large swaths of money committed to increasingly risky investments/loans that rely on asset appreciation just to be economically viable. A “unexpected” black swan that leads to default on interest payments snowballs into a rush for the exits as investors that were hungry for yield suddenly get cold feet and want their principal back, precipitating a financial crisis.

Put it another way, good times lead to investors overreaching for yield. Since no one can tell when the good times will end, this overreaching for yield accumulates risks that will go bad in a market downturn. This issue is multiplied by institutions that get some sort of fee based off volume (e.g. a percentage of assets under management or new originations) and are more concerned with meeting quarterly growth targets met by increasing bets in the risky sector until the last investment dollar is squeezed dry. These institutions and the people that run them are less worried about losses accumulating as they will have made large fees and profits during the good times. This is encapsulated in the famous quote from Citibank then-CEO Chuck Prince in July 2007 “as long as the music is on, we’re still dancing”. When the bad times inevitably hit, investors and eventually the public are left holding the bag.

Subprime crisis of 2007-2008, then

Before we get into the current day private credit market, let’s flashback to the 2007-2008 subprime crisis (which then morphed into the Great Financial Crisis of 2008) and see Minsky’s theory in action.

Let’s start with two charts below. The first chart shows how interest rates were slashed from 6.5% to 1% after the dotcom bubble burst. The second chart shows how housing prices had been steadily appreciated for several decades. An often heard quote back in 2006 was that there has never been a year where housing prices declined nationally in the US (I’m not sure how far back people were looking at that time as housing prices probably did decline during the Great Depression, some studies suggest this was a valid belief during 1975-2006).

Low interest rates and an appreciating mainstream asset coupled together to create an explosion in mortgage origination activity and money flooded into the subprime sector (i.e. loans to borrowers with low credit scores). While this may have worked on a small scale as in 1994, only $35 billion in subprime mortgages were originated (about 104,000 loans), by 2007 there were 7.5 million subprime loans outstanding (accounting for about 14% of mortgages at that time), with another 8-10% of “near-prime” loans. Buffett calls it the three Is, the innovator, the imitator and the idiot. The innovator may have found an opportunity in mispriced subprime loans in the 1990s, which was massively replicated by the imitators in the 2000s when interest rates were near zero and housing was appreciated.

Long story short, companies deep in the subprime trough went bust left and right. If we look back, 2008 seems like just a blip in the stock market’s upward trajectory. Ironically, even some of the subprime assets might not have been that bad. I remember when the Federal Reserve purchased $30 billion in mortgage assets from Bear Stearns in 2008 to provide liquidity. By the 2010s, the Federal Reserve actually managed to exit these assets at a profit (though probably in part due to the monetary easing creating a conducive environment in those years).

Alas but this was too late for many of the big names in subprime. Bear Stearns’ stock price fell to $2/share, while AIG is still trading at a fraction of where it was 2008. Value lost by shareholders/investors in the companies most exposed to subprime was real.

A major reason in my opinion is leverage. Subprime products were often leveraged in several ways: they were divided into tranches by risk (senior AAA tranches) and mezzanine and equity tranches which absorbed losses first. Then CDOs would repackage mezzanine tranches into senior AAA tranches and riskier mezzanine tranches. And given the underlying assets (subprime mortgages) typically had very high loan-to-values (LTV), you can see how these products were inherently fragile. In fact, had there been no leverage and banks just held an unleveraged subprime mortgage to maturity, there might not be much losses in the long run – using Shiller US national home price index as a proxy, 2008 was 180, now it’s 330, so home prices increased by 83%. Even if all the loans defaulted and the banks repossessed all the property, it’d be up 83% by now and probably could cover the loans. As Warren Buffett said, there are three Ls in losing money, leverage, liquor and I’ll omit the last L so it doesn’t sound misogynist, but he emphasized leverage.

Private Credit, Now

Back from our detour down the memory lane to the present day. Private credit has expanded rapidly since the Global Financial Crisis, growing from around $300 billion AUM in 2007 to nearly $2.5 trillion by 2023 (as of now, this figure is estimated by many sources to be near $3.5 trillion).

Let’s take a deep dive into one of the biggest names in the private credit space. Note that by analyzing this asset management company and its listed BDC, I am not necessarily thinking they are doing better or worse than any other companies in the field, but they are prominent and focused in this sector, so any analysis will be more relevant to private credit than some big asset manager that only has dabbled few percentage of its AUM in private credit.

Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL)

OWL is one of the biggest names in private credit with $300 billion AUM (of which about $158 billion AUM is in credit platforms, e.g. direct lending). Its stock price has fallen a lot throughout 2025 as investors' worries over OWL grew.

It mainly earns investment management fees from its funds. It has managed to grow its revenues from $200 million in 2019-2020 to $2.9 billion in 2025.

Let’s take a look at its biggest listed platform Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) to see what’s under the hood (below is from 10-k)

As at December 2025

Balance

Corresponding Income statement (FY2025)

Assets

$16.4 billion

Interest & investment income: $1.8 billion

Debt

$9.3 billion

Interest expense: -$0.6 billion

Net assets

$7.4 billion

FY2025

Income statement

Interest & investment income

$1.8 billion

Interest expense

-$0.6 billion

Management & incentive fees

-$0.4 billion

Net investment income (before fair value changes)

$0.8 billion

Source:10-K

So what we see is that OBDC earns $1.8 billion interest and investment income off $16.4 billion in assets, about 11%. Return on equity (using net investment income divided by net assets) is about 11% as well. Interest expense is roughly at 6.5% of debt.

But what if OBDC were entirely unlevered, assuming management and incentive fees changed proportionately with interest and investment income?

FY25

Interest & investment income

$0.8 billion ($7.4 billion net assets *11%)

Management & incentive fees

-$0.2 billion (FY25 0.4 billion fees/1.8 revenues*0.8 billion revenues))

Net investment income

$0.6 billion

Source: 10-K

The ROE would declined to about 8%, which would be at comparable levels with high yield bonds (one reference index puts the effective yield of high yield bonds at 7.14%). So effectively OBDC is juicing up returns with leverage so it generates 11% ROE on paper (and a higher management fee for OWL as compared to if unleveraged), otherwise it would look very similar to a high-yield bond ETF. This business model has worked very well for OWL. OBDC assets grew from $1 billion in Dec-2016 to $17 billion by Dec-25 and OWL’s assets under management and fees have grown as well.

On the other hand, OBDC’s underlying assets don’t seem to me to be too bad:

  • it basically makes loans to middle market companies (e.g. associated with during takeovers by private equity).
  • As of December 31, 2025, the portfolio companies “had weighted average annual revenue of $1.01 billion, weighted average annual EBITDA of $237 million, an average interest coverage of 1.9x and an average net loan-to value of 42%”. So there is some margin of safety embedded in these deals, though OBDC caveats that “The debt in which we invest typically is not rated by any rating agency, but if these instruments were rated, they would likely receive a rating of below investment grade (that is, below BBB- or Baa3), which is often referred to as “high yield” or “junk.””
  • The investments are also spread across a wide range of industries, even though software has the highest concentration, it is only 11.1% of total.

There’s always going to be a bit of irrational exuberance during upcycles, but OBDC’s underlying assets appear to me to be better than the subprime loans being made during the 2000s (such as NINJA (no income, no job or assets) loans). Even though the private credit segment has been facing redemptions and forced to put a cap on these redemptions, thereby fueling more investor angst, this is still somewhat different from the subprime crisis where banks owned oodles of subprime mortgage securities, were taking write-offs and people were worried about which bank would go under next. In the private credit universe, some companies may do poorly and even go under, but it’s all ringfenced amongst the investors in those securities and while it may dampen investor enthusiasm for a few quarters or even years, the impact is much more concentrated and narrow than the subprime crisis and the next time the Fed embarks on an easing cycle, investors will gradually forget about it and move on to the next big thing.

Nonetheless, I would not suggest new investment into OWL or OBDC or other private credit instruments and related companies. It is very difficult to know when these cycles bottom, while many saw there was something going on the subprime mortgages, few could predict it would end with Lehman’s bankruptcy and a whole string of other companies — Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA) aka Fannie Mae, American International Group, Inc. (AIG), etc — losing tremendous amounts of value for shareholders.

When Retail Goes In…

What might have been a good idea on a small scale was widely replicated via Buffett’s 3Is in the past decade, with even retail participation.

I’m a bit skeptical of “private equity” opportunities offered to retail investors:

  • retail investors are not offered the chance to invest in some of the biggest tech names during their growth phase before they go public, e.g. retail investors will only be able to buy into SpaceX at a reported IPO valuation of $1.75 trillion, after private investors have already benefited from most of the low-hanging fruit
  • private equity management companies going public have actually historically been signs of market peaks as excellently captured below. While this does not have anything to do with private credit directly, it’s similar in essence. Private equity makes money off deals and I’d be wary if they are selling you their a piece of their deal-making prowess.

  • and yet, in recent years we’ve heard about “private” opportunities becoming more open to retail investors which piques my wariness rather than enthusiasm as retail investors are not getting a piece of the action for SpaceX but somehow are offered PE fund buy-out loans in a leveraged structure such as OBDC.

Conclusion

If we look at OWL and OBDC as representative of the private credit sector, it does not appear to as systemic and pervasive a risk to the broader market as subprime mortgages were. The headlines will look scary, a few leveraged investments may go bad, but it’ll largely be contained within the investors of those instruments (which will have to sit through a few agonizing years hoping the money they invested eventually comes back and some may suffer losses).

How bad could it be?

  • Let's use the historical annual average default rate of 4.1% for speculative grade fixed income investments from S&P, and let's assume a 50% loss recovery.
  • Let's assume 5 years' of losses (as private credit grew a lot in the past 5 years) are concentrated in 1 or 2 years as people clamor for redemptions and credit tightens.
  • Back of the envelope math suggests 4%*50%*5= about 10% losses, multiplied by $3.5 trillion private credit AUM, would be about $350 billion in losses (c.1% of US nominal GDP of $30 trillion) By comparison, subprime loan losses were $1 trillion during 2007-2009 (7% of US nominal GDP of $14 trillion in 2007). Private credit would have lose $2 trillion (or about 57% of total AUM) in order to have the same magnitude impact as subprime and even if I don't find products like OBDC attractive, I don't think they are going to suffer anywhere near an average 57% loss on assets across the industry in every product.
  • To sum up, it'll probably be another blip in the credit cycle where inflows slow down for a few quarters as investors nurse their losses, but even if a few hundred billion dollars are lost, it's not going to be the end of the world.

However, I think retail investors should be very cautious in investing in these instruments as OBDC appears to be using leverage to increase returns which would otherwise be only somewhat better than a high yield bond. Even if say 10% of the loan value eventually are not recovered, with 2X leverage that's a 20% hit to capital. If investor appetite dries up, OWL's revenues could fall significantly. As the subprime crisis illustrated, permanent losses for investors can happen to specific companies in the impacted sector (especially with high leverage), so individual investors might want to think twice before deploying capital to this sector.

In my opinion, the best way for retail investors to play this is not to do anything in this sector and buy broader market equities, such as (SPY) or (QQQ) or financial sector ETF (XLF) if there is actually a "private credit crisis" blared all over the news as markets seize up on short term liquidity shortage, while in the long run (as in the subprime crisis) the Fed will print to ease any liquidity shortage.

data.title: Climb Bio: 'Buy' On Budoprutug Phase 2 Advancement pMN And Expansions Underway
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Climb Bio, Inc. (CLYM), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Eliem Therapeutics: Year End 2024 MN Data With Further Potential To Unlock Value." Before going further on this biotech, it is important to highlight that in that article I mentioned that the company had acquired Tenet Medicines and also had obtained a $120 million private placement agreement to carry it forward. The reason why I bring this up is because it decided to switch over its pipeline afterwards only to advance budoprutug [TNT119] and other drugs for the treatment of patients with autoimmune disorders. The end result is that it changed its name from Eliem Therapeutics to Climb Bio because of this strategic move on October 3, 2024.

It has been a while since I updated this biotech, but at that time I had given it a Buy rating based on its potential to develop the use of its anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody [mAb] budoprutug for the treatment of patients with primary membranous nephropathy [pMN] in an ongoing phase 1b study. More about the clinical data below, but the company did end up actually releasing positive results from its phase 1b study using this drug to treat these autoimmune disorder patients. Even better, it is currently in the process of evaluating the use of budoprutug in the ongoing phase 2 PrisMN trial treating these patients with pMN. Why do I believe it is important to go over this biotech now? That's because there are actually several catalysts or inflection points that are rapidly approaching. Initial data from the PrisMN trial is expected in the 2nd half of 2026.

That's not all, either; this drug is also being explored in other studies targeting patients with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus [SLE] and immune thrombocytopenia [ITP] as well, with clinical readouts from both of these programs anticipated in the 2nd half of 2026 as well. Another item to mention is that budoprutug is versatile in the sense that the company wants to shift it away eventually from intravenous [IV] dosing over to a subcutaneous [SC] formulation. The purpose is to provide patients with a self-administered at-home treatment option eventually. Data from a phase 1 healthy volunteer study evaluating the SC formulation of budoprutug are expected in the 1st half of 2026. With budoprutug still pressing forward in clinical testing targeting patients with pMN, plus several data readouts expected ahead, I believe it is important to maintain a Buy rating on this stock at this time.

Primary Membranous Nephropathy Indication Program Continues To Deliver Necessary Data

As I mentioned above, the company changed its name to Climb Bio, and the reason for doing so was to shift its focus for its pipeline to the targeting of patients with autoimmune disorders. With that being said, the company was indeed able to report positive results from its phase 1b study using budoprutug for the treatment of patients with primary membranous nephropathy [pMN]. It was even able to exhibit long-term data showing control of proteinuria in 4 patients three years after initial dosing [with a total of 4 doses]. It is even in the process of running the phase 2 open-label, dose-range-finding PrisMN study using this anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody to treat pMN patients who have persistent proteinuria despite optimized renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system [RAAS] inhibition. Before going over this entire pMN treatment program in detail, plus any catalysts to come about from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Primary Membranous Nephropathy [pMN] is characterized as an autoimmune disorder of the kidney. Specifically, the immune system [primary anti-PLA2R antibodies] attacks the glomeruli [tiny filters] of the kidney. Why is that bad? That's because these filters are responsible for filtering out waste from the blood of a patient. Without these being effective, there are two main issues that these patients experience:

  • Reduced kidney function
  • Protein in the urine.

With respect to the second one, protein in the urine, a little bit is okay. However, these patients can experience something known as Nephrotic Syndrome, which is protein in the urine above 3.5 g/day. This is a cascading effect because then this syndrome can lead to swelling in multiple parts of a person's body. The majority, 3 out of 4 people with MN, have pMN, which is what this company is going after. The other type, Secondary Membranous Nephropathy is a result of an infection and/or other autoimmune disorder like lupus, possibly. The global membranous nephropathy market is expected to reach $590 million by 2035. This initial market is not as big as other multibillion-dollar markets, but there is something of importance to mention here. Should the company be successful in developing budoprutug for pMN, it could potentially have the whole market to itself. The reason why is because there are no FDA-approved therapies for this rare kidney disease.

These patients desperately need a new treatment option to control proteinuria and the many other problems. However, there is something more important to note, which is that uncontrolled pMN disease means the patient is moving on to developing chronic kidney disease or end-stage kidney disease. The best that patients could hope for would be the current 1st-line treatment option for them, which is rituximab. There are two problems with this treatment option, though. The first of which is that about between 20% and 40% of patients who receive it are refractory [Climb Bio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #14].

Secondly, it is only given as an off-label option. As I mentioned above, the beauty of budoprutug is that it can be applied to many disorders. The other two disorders that the company is looking at treating with this drug are SLE and ITP, both of which would offer expansion potential. This is only made possible, though, because of the fact that this anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody has broad coverage. That is, it is able to deplete pathogenic B-cells across the entire B-cell lineage [Climb Bio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #11]. Including that of Ab-secreting B cells, which are plasmablasts and plasma cells. With its 100X enhanced antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity [ADCC] and its strong binding affinity to CD19, this drug is well equipped to help treat pMN patients.

The thing is that this company has been able to show this drug to actually work well in treating these patients. This would be with both phase 1b data and long-term data. The phase 1b data was ideal because the final outcome was that 100% [5 out of 5] ended up achieving rapid and durable B-cell depletion with a dose of budoprutug ranging from 100 mg to 200 mg. This is crucial because it goes back to what I stated above, which is that B-cell depletion is necessary to achieve desired data. This is exactly what transpired in that the final outcome was that all 5 patients had also achieved either a complete remission or partial remission in the same study at Week 48. This was measured to look at proteinuria, known as Urine Protein Creatine Ratio [UPCR]. The second aspect to consider here would be long-term data. Climb Bio also delivered on this as well because it was noted that pMN patients were able to achieve long-term control of proteinuria for 3 years after initial dosing [up to 4 doses given for them].

Furthermore, the notion is that 3 of these 4 patients no longer required immunosuppressive therapy either. Why is this finding important? That's because such therapy is given to these patients to prevent attacks from anti-PLA2R that damage the kidney's filtration barrier.

It remains to be seen what happens with this program, but it definitely is an unmet medical need. That is why the company also achieved another piece of good news lately, which is that the FDA granted budoprutug for the treatment of patients with pMN with Fast Track Designation. This is because of the fact that there are no therapies approved for these patients, plus the early data observed to date from it showing the potential to be a disease-modifying therapy for them.

In order to see whether or not Climb Bio is going to be able to effectively use this drug to treat these patients, it is evaluating its use in the ongoing phase 2 open-label, dose-range-finding PrisMN study. The thing is that there are two criteria for the patients that are being recruited into this study. The first of which is that they have to be anti-PLA2R antibody-positive. Secondly, they must have persistent proteinuria despite optimized RAAS inhibition. Such pMN patients who still have ≥1 g/day despite RASS inhibition are at greater risk for kidney failure. What is this inhibition? Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system [RAAS] inhibitors are important for reducing intracapillary glomerular pressure and offering size selectivity as well. Both of these events are supposed to help reduce proteinuria. The company is going to treat these patients in a dose-escalation design as follows:

  • 15 patients on low-dose
  • 15 patients mid-dose
  • 15 patients on high dose.

Each of these cohorts is to be given IV budoprutug as two doses 14 days apart. From there, they are to receive a maintenance dose at 24 weeks [two doses again]. From there, they will be evaluated for the main efficacy endpoint of complete and partial remission at Week 48 [Both UPCR and eGFR are being deployed for this endpoint]. As I mentioned above, what's also important to track would be biomarker data. This deals with depleted B-cells, anti-PLA2R negativity, and immunoglobulin. With that being said, Climb Bio expects to release data from the phase 2 PrisMN trial of budoprutug treating patients with pMN in the 2nd half of 2026.

The main thing to note with the data is that it is only going to be initial data. This means the intention is to release B-cell and anti-PLA2R data only. Again, the drug can be used for other autoimmune disorders. With that being said, data from phase 1b studies using this anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody to treat patients with SLE and ITP are expected in the 2nd half of 2026 as well. Plus, the company is already thinking about how it could provide a competitive advantage with respect to dosing. It is evaluating this drug in a phase 1 human volunteer study to test an SC formulation of budoprutug in terms of pharmacokinetic [PK], pharmacodynamic [PD], and bioavailability profiles. Although, the truth is that this company already has a highly competitive profile to begin with [Climb Bio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #20].

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC filing, Climb Bio had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $160.7 million as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this biotech is in great shape in terms of its cash on hand. The reason why I state this is because in its Q4 and full-year 2025 financial results, it mentions that it believes its cash on hand is enough to fund its operations into 2028. Thus, I don't believe that there will be the need to raise cash in the short term.

With that said, it is also important to note that there are several catalysts expected to happen during this year. This means that if positive results are released, causing the stock price to trade considerably higher, then it is my opinion that management might take advantage of this and raise cash anyway. There even is a financial tool already in place for this company at its disposal, should it choose to utilize it. This would be an Equity Distribution Agreement with Oppenheimer, whereby the company could sell from time to time up to an aggregate of $22.4 million.

The company burns about $19.3 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $13.7 million in R&D expenses and then $5.6 million in G&A expenses.

Risks to Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Climb Bio. The first risk to consider would be in terms of its lead program, which is using budoprutug for the treatment of patients with pMN in the ongoing phase 2 PrisMN study. As I have gone over above, partial or complete remission was observed in 100% of patients [5 out of 5] at Week 48. The same primary endpoint of remission will also be evaluated in the ongoing PrisMN trial.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that the remissions observed in the phase 1b study will be again observed in the next phase 2 study. Especially since there was a small sample size released from the phase 1b study.

The second risk to consider would be the expansion opportunities that the company is going after with its development of anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody budoprutug. It is looking to see if the drug would work for other autoimmune disorders like SLE and ITP. The risk is that even though the drug was shown to do well in terms of B-cell depletion and efficacy in treating patients with pMN, there is no guarantee that it will do well in the targeting of these other two disorders. Nor that the data to be released in the 2nd half of 2026 from both of these indications will be enough to move forward with the next stage of clinical testing.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of dosing options for budoprutug. The reason why is because the company has been able to do quite well using an IV version of this drug to treat pMN. The hope is that it is going to be able to also develop a potent SC formulation for patients, one that could be offered as a self-administered at-home treatment option to treat their autoimmune disorders. Data from a phase 1 healthy volunteer study using an SC version of budoprotug is expected in the 1st half of 2026.

The risk here is that there is no assurance this SC formulation of the drug will be safe/tolerable, for starters. The company expects to release B-cell depletion data as well. Thus, another risk is that even though this drug has been set up to have B-cell depletion across the entire lineage of B-cells, there is no guarantee that final B-cell depletion data will end up meeting or exceeding expectations.

Conclusion

Eliem Therapeutics had decided to change its name over to Climb Bio. This move came about after it had acquired Tenet Medicines and also achieved a $120 million private placement agreement. The reason for the name change is to indicate a change in being able to help improve options for patients with autoimmune disorders. Its lead candidate, anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody budoprutug, is being developed in the ongoing phase 2 PrisMN study targeting pMN patients with persistent proteinuria despite optimized renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system [RAAS] inhibition.

Data from this study is expected to be released in the 2nd half of 2026. The beauty of this company is its ability to apply budoprutug to treat other B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders [Climb Bio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #7]. As you can see from this slide, the possibilities for it beyond SLE and ITP are endless. Lastly, it is in the process of developing the anti-APRIL monoclonal antibody CLYM116 for the treatment of patients with IgA Nephropathy [IgAN].

This is being evaluated in a phase 1 healthy volunteer study and is looking at several parameters like safety, PK, and PD profile of this drug. Initial healthy volunteer data from this study is expected in mid-2026. With both positive phase 1b and long-term data achieved using budoprutug for the treatment of patients with pMN, plus a data readout from the phase 2 PrisMN study for this same program in the 2nd half of 2026, I believe that I should maintain this stock with a Buy rating.

data.title: Adagene Looks Attractive With ADG126 And Validating Partnerships
data.description:
Adagene Inc. (ADAG) is a clinical-stage oncology biotech that develops antibody-based immunotherapies. ADAG’s main asset is ADG126 (muzastotug), which uses its masking technology (SAFEbody) that is designed to reduce systemic toxicity to provide a potentially better safety profile. The company will update data on efficacy and tolerability results at the upcoming American Association for Cancer Research Annual Meeting 2026 (AACR 2026). ADAG also collaborates with Sanofi (SNY) and Incyte (INCY), which support ADG126’s R&D as well. Sanofi has committed up to $25 million for its Phase 2 in MSS CRC. ADAG will supply ADG126 for another SNY study evaluating the drug in advanced solid tumors. As such, I believe ADAG’s underlying science with ADG126 and compelling valuation warrant a speculative “Buy” rating for long-term investors at these levels.

ADG126: Novel Immuno-Oncology Bet

Adagene Inc. is a clinical-stage biotechnology company that develops cancer immunotherapies based on antibodies. ADAG was incorporated in the Cayman Islands back in 2011. It's the parent company of a subsidiary, Adagene Incorporated, registered in Delaware in 2017. However, the business itself is headquartered in Suzhou, Jiangsu Province, China. Interestingly, the ADSs have appreciated by about 150% from their 2026 lows, which is why I thought it was worthwhile covering this name.

As a quick overview, ADAG’s pipeline includes its main asset, ADG126, also called muzastotug. This is a masked antibody, which is studied in Phase 2 for microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer (MSS CRC) in patients without current or active liver metastases in combination with Keytruda, which is produced by Merck (MRK). A masked antibody is designed to stay inactive until it reaches the tumor environment, where certain enzymes remove the mask and activate it.

Moreover, ADG126 targets CTLA-4, an immune checkpoint that restrains T-cell activation, aiming to release the immune system against cancer. However, the same mechanism also unleashes immune activity in normal tissues, producing toxicity. In principle, this is why a masked antibody is useful. After all, it can remain inactive while circulating in healthy tissues and only activates in the tumor environment, which helps avoid any potential adverse effects. For now, this is a purely theoretical approach, but if ADAG’s trials prove it can work as intended, it could unlock a sizeable market.

Additionally, a safer anti-CTLA-4 could potentially be dosed effectively combined with programmed death receptor-1 (PD-1), like Keytruda, or other agents. The company claims that the masking enables 20x higher dosing over ipilimumab, produced by Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY). Notably, ADG126 obtained a fast track designation by the FDA for treatments that combine it with Keytruda.

Pipeline Potential and Partnerships

Moreover, ADG126 is designed to enhance antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) and may potentially be used for multiple tumor types. This is because many tumors use immune suppression and regulatory T cells (Tregs) to protect themselves from immune attack. The company is exploring the development of the treatment of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and advanced solid tumors.

ADAG’s pipeline also contains two other preclinical assets. First, ADG138 is IND-ready for HER2-expressing solid tumors. The other drug is ADG152, but this one is in the IND-enabling phase for CD20-positive blood cancers, especially B-cell malignancies such as lymphoma. However, both of these assets are masked T cell engagers, meaning that they are engineered antibodies that bind two different targets at once. One arm binds the tumor target, and the other arm binds CD3 on T cells, aiming to physically bring T cells close to cancer cells to kill them.

With that in mind, ADAG plans to update its clinical data at the American Association for Cancer Research Annual Meeting 2026 (AACR 2026) for the combination of ADG126 with Keytruda. ADAG also expects to report initial data for other combo therapies (ADG126+Keytruda+fruquintinib) in late-line MSS CRC. Additionally, ADAG’s AACR 2026 report will include its results for atezolizumab+bevacizumab in 1L hepatocellular carcinoma. These drugs are produced by Roche (RHHBY) and commercialized through its subsidiary Genentech. Lastly, ADAG plans an initial Phase 2 readout for ADG126+Keytruda in neoadjuvant CRC. This treatment is intended for patients prior to tumor surgery.

On top of that, ADAG’s proprietary platform is SAFEbody, an antibody-masking system, which is one part of the company’s Dynamic Precision Library that also includes NEObody, for antibody design, and POWERbody, which combines SAFEbody masking with other modalities such as T-cell engagers. Interestingly, ADAG is working in partnership with Incyte (INCY) to evaluate ADG126 in combination with INCY’s TGFßR2xPD-1, specifically INCA33890. This drug is in clinical trials for the treatment of MSS CRC, and INCY has reported promising Phase 1 results.

Nevertheless, ADAG’s most important partnership is probably with Sanofi (SNY), which will invest up to $25 million to support a Phase 2 trial of ADG126 with pembrolizumab. Also, ADAG will supply ADG126 for an SNY-sponsored trial in Phase 1/2 for the treatment of advanced solid tumors. SNY had the right to evaluate early SAFEbody discovery work and chose to activate a third drug-discovery program using this technology. That decision triggered a contractual payment to ADAG, and if the program progresses successfully, ADAG could eventually receive milestone payments and royalties.

Valuation and Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ADAG’s April 2026 6-K shows that as of December 2025, its balance sheet held $74.5 million in cash against $5.4 million in financial debt (aside from other operating liabilities). At the time, there were 59.2 million shares outstanding. However, we also know they recently raised another $65.3 million in net proceeds by issuing 18.7 million new ADSs. So, in total, the “updated” cash balances are likely closer to $139.8 million, which increases the total shares outstanding to approximately 82.6 million (or 66.1 million ADSs). Based on the current $3.80 ADS price, that implies a market cap of about $251.2 million.

Moreover, the 6-K shows book value was $35.4 million as of December 2025. But adjusting for the recently raised $65.3 million, it would work out to an “updated” book value of around $100.7 million. That would imply a relatively compelling P/B of 2.5. For comparison, the sector’s median P/B is 2.8. Having said that, ADAG doesn’t have any product revenues yet, even though it did record $7.7 million in licensing/collaboration revenues. Those revenues came from ADAG’s partnership deals with Sanofi, ConjugateBio, and Third Arc Bio.

Additionally, I calculate that ADAG burned through $15.7 million during 2025. Note that I got those figures by simply adding ADAG’s 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. This way, I estimate ADAG would have an extremely comfortable 9-year cash runway, which would seem pretty healthy for a biotech of this size. However, I anticipate ADAG’s cash burn will very likely ramp up considerably. After all, they have to complete the ADG126+Pembrolizumab randomized Phase 2 trial enrollment while continuing with the Phase 1b/2 trial on MSS CRC.

In fact, ADAG’s SEC filing explicitly says they’ll use the proceeds for R&D on ADG138 and other programs, so it’s not only for ADG126. It’s challenging to pin down a more precise burn rate. Though I’ll point out that pre-raise, ADAG did say they had enough funding ($74.5 million at the time) until 2028. And post-raise ADAG has almost twice that amount ($139.8 million); my rough runway estimate would be into late 2028 to early 2029.

Therefore, I think ADAG’s bull case ultimately rests on whether or not ADG126 succeeds or fails. That means, as long as their future clinical trial updates continue to be positive, the stock is very likely to start re-rating much higher. After all, even ADG126’s initial $1.0 billion TAM alone would be more than enough to justify ADAG’s current valuation. But, in the long run, ADAG would likely target 1L MSS CRC and Neoadjuvant demographics as well, which are much bigger markets.

Conversely, if we see ADG126’s randomized Phase 2 trial yields disappointing data, then the stock could easily head lower. Even if the efficacy is good, trials show it’s a candidate with serious side effects, which could also derail the bull case. So, the main drawback with ADAG is that it’s now mostly a bet on a single asset (i.e., ADG126). Aside from that, I think they have a reasonably good runway, which does derisk the overall risk-reward equation and nudges me towards a bullish rating this time.

Conclusion: Worth Speculating

Overall, ADAG is a promising biotech due to ADG126. They also have some interesting earlier-stage candidates as well, but I wouldn’t consider them the main value driver right now. In that sense, ADAG is essentially a bet on ADG126, which adds concentration risks to my bull thesis. But, given that they have a relatively well-funded balance sheet after the recent raise, I believe ADAG is now a viable speculative “Buy” for long-term investors seeking exposure to immuno-oncology high-risk, high-reward plays. Just make sure you keep your position sizing manageable until ADAG, as this is a relatively illiquid stock with only 106.4 thousand shares in average volume. So it’ll likely remain a volatile name for the foreseeable future.

data.title: Allbirds: AI Pivot Is A Fantasy With Limited Capital (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The art of the pivot was arguably perfected in Silicon Valley: when your business fails at one thing, find another thing that you can succeed at. A 180-degree pivot was on full display as Allbirds, Inc. (BIRD), a San Francisco company that raised nearly $350 million as a private company and briefly touched a $4 billion valuation upon IPO, announced its intention to pivot from being a shoemaker to a prospective GPU-as-a-service company named "NewBird AI."

The surprise announcement rocked the markets, and though Allbirds has dropped from recent highs notched immediately after the announcement, its share price has still gained ~fivefold since the deal was announced, adding roughly $80 million in market value. The question for investors now is, can the rally hold, and does NewBird AI really have a shot in this crowded new market?

I previously wrote a cautious, neutral article on Allbirds in 2023, when the stock was trading around $1 per share. First, an update on the fate of the shoe business: the parent company of Allbirds, now NewBird AI, agreed to sell the brand assets of Allbirds for $39 million to American Exchange Group, a brand portfolio that also owns other shoe brands, including Ecko, Jonathan Adler, and Aerosole.

I'll cut to the chase here: I have low confidence in Allbirds/NewBird AI's ability to succeed in a crowded GPU-as-a-service market. I'm dropping my rating on Allbirds to Sell.

Problem #1: Lack Of Leadership Experience Will Likely Mean Costly New Hires

Here's the first, most glaring issue that I see when it comes to Allbirds' pivot. While it's true that the GPU-as-a-service business is largely a financial endeavor rather than a technical one that requires real R&D chops, we have to state the obvious: as a shoe company, Allbirds has zero experience in being a neocloud company.

The snapshot below, taken from Allbirds' investor website, showcases the profiles of its top leadership.

The company's CEO, Joe Vernachio (CEO for the last five years, since 2021), is a retail executive through and through, previously serving as president of Mountain Hardware and, before that, as an executive at the North Face division of VF Corporation (VFC). Similarly, the company's CFO also has primarily retail experience, serving in top finance roles at Gymshark and Adidas.

Allbirds' previous business relied heavily on shoe design, supply chain operations, and retail footprint expansion. Becoming a neocloud requires an entirely different skill set: sales to enterprise customers, deep financial expertise that can raise creative financing for expensive GPUs, and real estate to house the datacenters.

We find it unlikely that Allbirds has the experience in-house to pull off this pivot, and we expect announcements on major (and costly) executive hires to deepen the management bench as it makes the transition.

Problem #2: Capital Sufficiency

We next have to ask another major question: does Allbirds have enough resources to go toe-to-toe in a business that is increasingly being dominated by large neocloud firms (CoreWeave (CRWV), IREN Ltd. (IREN), Nebius (NBIS), and Oracle (ORCL))?

Let's dig through all the pieces of Allbirds' liquidity. The last time the company reported earnings (and gave us a balance sheet) was for the September period.

As of September, the company had $23.7 million of cash on its books, offset against $12.3 billion of debt. Given the recent trend of sales declines (-23% y/y in Q3) and tariff-riddled gross margin decay, we expect that the period from September through March delivered further cash burn that likely takes down cash reserves to near-zero (for reference, the operating company's cash burn in Q4 '24 was -$10.9 million, and burn in Q1 '25 was -$27.9 million: we think it's unlikely that the company performed much better than that in Q4 '25-Q1 '26).

Next, there are the proceeds from the sale of the Allbirds brand to American Exchange Group. We note, however, that instead of reinvesting the proceeds into the new GPU business, the company is preparing to issue a special dividend, subject to shareholder approval at an upcoming shareholder meeting.

Assuming very little cash cushion from ongoing operations post-September and no residual proceeds from the sale of the Allbirds brand, we are only left with the brand-new $50 million convertible debt facility that the company just raised to pursue its AI ambitions.

Let's now compare this capital cushion against some of the better-known neocloud companies.

CoreWeave, which is the largest and best-known AI infrastructure company, recently issued guidance calling for a whopping $30-$35 billion in capex:

We note that CoreWeave is able to access tremendous amounts of capital to drive capex thanks to its use of pass-through financing facilities. These facilities enable CoreWeave to leverage the financial credit profile of its underlying customers (i.e., hyperscalers like Meta (META)) to determine its interest rate and borrowing capacity, rather than CoreWeave's own financial metrics. For example, the company just raised $8.5 billion in delayed draw term loans for a major client at an all-in interest rate below 6%.

Iren is another major GPU-as-a-service company, which recently pivoted from Bitcoin mining to rededicate its resources toward AI datacenter. Iren has $2.8 billion of cash on its books as of January 31st, as shown in the image below, with over $3.6 billion in additional GPU financing secured.

We don't need to draw on other examples in order to make this point: Allbirds' ~$50 million is peanuts in an industry that is being dominated by large amounts of capital. Allbirds' relatively small capital will only allow it to build a smaller datacenter, which can only service smaller clients (if any at all). This immediately handicaps Allbirds relative to companies like CoreWeave, whose blue-chip customer base allows it to access financing at much more favorable rates. This is an industry that favors those who are well capitalized: Allbirds is starting off with a major handicap.

Key Takeaways

Allbirds' AI announcement is flashy, but it lacks substance. In order to have a decent shot at being a successful GPU rental company, Allbirds will need to make strategic hires to give it the know-how to compete in the industry. This will be costly, on top of the company having very little capital and financing committed to date. It's dwarfed by the current pure-play neocloud companies, which have tremendously more capital and better access to financing versus Allbirds.

The next major catalyst for Allbirds is its next quarterly update on May 7. Sell this spike: there is little fundamental promise here.

data.title: Nebius: Did Meta And Microsoft Buy Compute At Near-Zero Margin? (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last year, I upgraded Nebius (NBIS) to a "Buy" after its first (and transformative) deals with Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT).

However, looking at NBIS's profit margins after the initial racks came online, I am starting to question how these contracts were priced. Meta's capacity under the terms of the deal has been fully deployed and online as of January 2026. The first tranche of MSFT's racks became online last November, with most of the remaining capacity coming online in the second half of 2026.

There are early signs that NBIS might have been accepting extremely low (or even unprofitable) margins on its raw-compute contracts with hyperscalers in order to scale and pursue its dream of becoming a full-stack cloud provider itself.

The first step of pursuing this dream is building the physical infrastructure. The coming paragraphs examine the motives of hyperscalers supporting a potential competitor.

I am downgrading Nebius to a "Hold" until more clarity on the unit economics of its raw compute deals with Meta and Microsoft is revealed in the coming quarters.

Growth

Nebius' fast rise and recent US expansion have attracted attention from investors. The company reported FY'25 growth of 479% YoY, with revenue reaching $530 million just ~18 months after inception in mid 2024.

Demand for GPUs from hyperscalers is a key driver for this growth. NBIS gets a solid allocation of Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs. During the Q4'25 earnings call last February, management highlighted that its GPU base is in the hundreds of thousands, and while hyperscalers have more GPUs, the count is still measured in the hundreds of thousands as well, putting NBIS in the same class as hyperscalers. Meta and Microsoft signed deals worth billions. NBIS expects to end 2026 with an Annual Revenue Run Rate "ARR" between $6 to $9 billion.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates NBIS a solid A on growth, with working capital being a key drag against a perfect A+ rating. Demand for compute is high but costly to build. NBIS says it is particularly focused on maintaining both a solid balance sheet and avoiding dilution, leaning on customers for prepayments in addition to revenue to fund growth. These dynamics do impact working capital growth, as mentioned above, but simultaneously set NBIS apart from other compute providers such as IREN (IREN) and Applied Digital (APLD), but at par with CoreWeave (CRWV).

If my hypothesis is true, we should see an increasing divergence in the balance sheets and unit economics of raw-compute-only data center REIT providers on one hand and neo-clouds with full-stack aspirations on the other.

Balance Sheet

NBIS is an infrastructure operator with a balance sheet of a tech company with just ~$500 million in net debt. For a company running $5.5 billion in Properties, Plants and Equipment "PPE" and putting the groundwork for a further $16 billion to $20 billion of additional CapEx, that's a modest debt.

Management says that it plans to use more debt to fund its CapEx. That's a good corporate finance practice. But, overall, the debt ratio is modest, with only 62% of assets funded via debt. Equally important, NBIS says that equity raises will continue to be limited.

One way management funds its infrastructure buildout is by asking for pre-payments from its customers. Deferred revenue stood at $1.6 billion as of December 2025.

Management delivered all the capacity under its contract with Meta. In 2026, it will be installing GPU racks under the Microsoft contract. In total, NBIS expects CapEx between $16 billion and $20 billion ($18 billion at midpoint), with management noting that 60% of funding secured via multiple arrangements and balance sheet accounts.

Margins

NBIS's Q4'25 margins tie all the pieces of the investment thesis together, highlighting the risks and opportunities. Simply put, NBIS's AI infrastructure business isn't profitable. The risk is that management doesn't have plans to become profitable in the short term. This is serious because I am talking about profitability on a contract-level, not because of temporary items related to growth or revenue ramp. This could be more fundamental.

The math of infrastructure projects is simple. The lifespan of a GPU across the industry is 4-6 years. (Note: Starting January 2026, NBIS will assume five years for depreciation, up from four years). As more power-efficient chips come to market, it becomes less logical to use older, more power-hungry chips, given the power shortage and running costs. A typical 8 GPU computing node, such as DGX B200, sells for roughly half a million dollars. Excluding installation, cables, cooling, interconnects, etc, the breakeven contract price of this node should be $100,000 per year for the GPU hardware ($500,000 over a 5-year lifespan).

The question now is, why is NBIS unprofitable? Management's own adjusted net income, designed to reflect the net income run rate by excluding non-recurring gains/expenses, stood at a negative $173 million in Q4'25.

Note: Some analysts highlight that NBIS is profitable on a cash basis, ignoring depreciation, the largest and only link between CapEx and returns. That's an erroneous analysis. The lifespan assumption could also be used to attack the unprofitability thesis. If there is a discrepancy between the actual life of the chips and their accounting lifespan used to calculate depreciation, NBIS GAAP accounting might underestimate actual profits, and cash basis accounting becomes important. We examine this scenario in more detail in the risk section.

What's Nebius's End Game?

If NBIS's contracts aren't profitable, then why do they do what they do? I believe that NBIS is playing the long game here. They want to become a public cloud hyperscaler (like Google Cloud, AWS, and Azure). However, the market is difficult to penetrate. To become a hyperscaler, you need data centers across the world. That would be expensive. Thus, they offer hyperscalers an attractive price. While the contract pricing doesn't cover the entire infrastructure buildout, it does cover most of it. Hyperscalers, on the other hand, are willing to support a potential competitor for two reasons. First, there is a shortage in GPUs, and NVDA is supporting neo-Clouds such as NBIS by allocating GPUs in response to hyperscalers attacking its own turf by developing their own chips (AMZN runs AI compute on Trainium and Inferentia, while Meta and MSFT have MITA and Maia and other proprietary microchip programs. Secondly, the price discount offered by NBIS is hard to ignore.

Risks

One might argue that the GPU lifespan is more than five years, citing the recent reports that demand for H100 has pushed compute prices on these devices up. NBIS notes that capacity for its older chips is sold out, and unlike what analysts expected, compute prices have remained firm. That might be true, but these are exceptional circumstances because of the computer shortage. Users are using what they've got. However, I can imagine some applications that are okay with a lower token per second per watt if the price is right.

Secondly, a long-term upside risk is that NBIS might eventually succeed in becoming a public cloud provider, at least important enough to have a meaningful market share in this expansive, high-margin business.

Final Thoughts

A rating downgrade is warranted after NBIS reported persistent and deep losses in the quarters subsequent to the ramp-up in sales on the back of its deals with Meta and MSFT. There is a chance that this hypothesis is wrong, and that profitability might be due to a ramp in revenue (think overhead costs tied to construction and deal arrangements). However, typically, when this is the case, management makes it perfectly clear on earnings calls. This didn't happen in Nebius's case. Instead, management emphasized how non-core its deals with Meta and MSFT are. I will not wait and speculate on unit economics. Until things become clearer in the coming quarters, I am downgrading NBIS to "Hold".

data.title: Stellantis: Alliance With Chinese Automaker Leapmotor Points To A Long, Tough Climb
data.description:
Stellantis N.V., following a dreadful 2025 that included a $26.3 billion annual net loss, faces a long, steep and difficult recovery to remain viable in the face of rising competition from the Chinese automotive industry and uncertainty over regulation governing electrification in major markets.

The automaker’s guidance for 2026, issued in late February, is relatively vague:

“a mid-single-digit percent increase in net revenues, a low-single-digit AOI margin, and improved industrial free cash flow generation year over year. Sequential improvement is also expected from the first half to the second half of the year.”

An early green shoot – not yet indicative of too much – is Stellantis’ 4% first-quarter increase in its U.S. unit sales against the backdrop of a domestic industry that reported an approximate 6% market decline. Following a business reset in the U.S. with new leadership and a pivot favoring the company’s strongest nameplates, sales were boosted by the Ram pickup truck and the Jeep division.

Production bump

On April 15, Stellantis announced a 12% YOY global shipment increase of vehicles to dealers, a total of about 1.36 million units. Shipments were up significantly across all major regions. In the U.S., the increase was driven by demand for Ram 1500 Hemi V8, refreshed Jeep Grand Wagoneer and the all-new Jeep Cherokee, Stellantis said.

General Motors Company (GM) and Ford Motor Company (F), which had invested heavily into battery electric vehicles (BEVs), both saw significant unit declines for the quarter in the U.S., evidence that Stellantis has the ability to claw back lost market share against its domestic rivals.

Stellantis had been developing BEVs at a slower pace than its U.S. rivals. Following the exit of previous CEO Carlos Tavares and promotion of Antonio Filosa to the post, the automaker has adjusted its mix of propulsion offerings, emphasizing some powerful gasoline engines like the Hemi and accelerating development of extended range electrified vehicles (EREVs).

Historically known as a “muscle car” automaker in the U.S., Filosa appears bent on restoring that reputation.

Ram 1500 REV is an EREV that offers 169 miles of range using battery only and 690 miles of range total when the on-board engine with a full tank of gasoline recharges the battery. EREV technology may provide a transition from gasoline-only engines to a future in which battery-only powertrains are seen by consumers as more practical alternatives.

Production is scheduled to begin for the Ram EREV model as well as a Jeep version later this year. The technology will be unfamiliar for most U.S. consumers; its reception by the market could be consequential for STLA’s financial performance.

European production, which accounts for about 48% of Stellantis’ total, was up 12% in the first quarter. The increase was driven by models based on Stellantis’ Smart Car platform, Fiat Grande Panda and the Opel/Vauxhall Frontera. According to Automotive News, Stellantis has been cutting European prices aggressively to reduce inventory, especially of slow-selling models. Peugeot e-208 small BEV hatchback is available to lease for 150 Euros a month, no money down.

At the annual meeting on April 14, Filosa told shareholders the company is taking “painful but necessary steps” to build market share, revenue and margin. Unrealistic optimism for BEV sales were a factor behind write downs underlying last year’s immense financial losses.

The latest “momentum is real,” Filosa said. Financial reporting on a quarterly basis, in contrast to the current semiannual basis, will begin April 30. Filosa is scheduled to present the company’s strategic plan in detail at an Investors Day on May 21.

Help from the east

Collaboration with the Chinese auto industry is taking shape as a key tenet of the company’s long-term strategy. Stellantis is in early talks with China’s Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology Co. (ZJLMF) – of which it owns a bit less than 20% -- to build an Opel-branded SUV, probably in Spain. The targeted output at Stellantis’s Zaragoza plant would be about 50,000 units, starting in 2028.

The talks also include the possibility of an Alfa Romeo BEV model at Zaragoza.

The collaborative approach now looks highly rational and likely what was envisioned when STLA spend 1.5 billion Euros to buy a 20% Leapmotor stake in 2023. At the time GM and Ford were investing multiple billions to forge their own BEV strategy and infrastructure; Stellantis lacked similar financial resources. In retrospect Stellantis was fortunate because much of what it might have invested probably would have produced very little in the way of profitable sales.

Ford has been the most open and candid about the extent to which Chinese automakers and battery manufacturers now lead the global automotive industry, in terms of performance and technology.

North American landing?

Stellantis and Leapmotor already have their eye on North America following Canada’s decision in January to lower the import tariff to 6.1% (compared to the U.S.’s more than 100% tariff) on BEVs imported from China. Stellantis owns an empty assembly plant in Brampton, Ontario that might be a local assembly site for Stellantis/Leapmotor BEVS if objections from Canadian politicians can be overcome.

Consumers are gradually migrating to BEVs in Europe and the North America, Stellantis’s two most important markets. The tricky part for management will be maintaining sufficient sales and profit growth from traditional models over the next decade or two to fund electrification. Even so, finances will be tight: new internal combustion design and digital tech will consume capital on top of the resources required for new BEV models.

The competitiveness of BEV models based on Leapmotor design and technology hasn’t been tested widely in Western markets against BYD (BYDDF), Geely (GELYF) and other large Chinese enterprises. In China, Leapmotor is known as a “value leader” for its aggressive low pricing; at the same time, it lacks the cachet of better known Chinese brands.

Many question marks remain for Stellantis and its Chinese partner, though the company is pointed in the right direction and showing early – very early – signs of recovery. I’ll stay with my Hold recommendation on STLA.
